{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|qq=|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|qq=|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== See also ==
* [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]]
* {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}}
* {{ill|Imperial Descendant Sekke|ja|皇別摂家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Descendant Sekke|qq=|1=Kōbetsu sekke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581001}}
* [[:en:Ōke|Former Imperial Family]]
* {{ill|Branch family|ja|家制度#分家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Branch family|qq=|1=ie|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1149315}}
* {{ill|Cadet branch|ja|庶家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cadet branch|qq=|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}}
* {{ill|Royal descendants|ja|王孫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal descendants|qq=|1=Royal descendants|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18926038}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book |editor=Shibayama Nori |year=1880 |title=Kazoku Classification: Volume 1 (Kōbetsu) |publisher=Heizan Shoin |id={{NDLJP |780644}}}}
* {{cite journal|author=Shinokawa Ken|title=The Formation of the Kabane System|journal=Journal of Common Culture Studies|volume=26|pages=35-59|year=2007|publisher=Seijo University}}
* {{cite book|author=Kuramoto Kazuhiro|title=Imperial Genji: The Noble Supporters of the Throne|publisher=Chuokoronsha|year=2019}}
* {{cite journal|author=Sugazawa Yoko|title=Clan Consciousness and the Shinsen Shōjiroku|journal=Historical Perspectives|volume=58|pages=209-220|year=2001}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', compiled by the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from [[:en:China|China]] and the [[:en:Korea|Korea]]). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]].{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor [[:en:Saga|Saga]] demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]], while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. In the [[:en:9th century|9th century]], the Kanmu Taira's [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] led the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Tengyō no Ran]], and the [[:en:Heike|Heike]] (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], and [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe]], [[:en:Ichijō family|Ichijō]], and [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa]] branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through [[:en:Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyatatehime]].
* [[:en:Ō clan|Ō clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Nitta clan|Nitta clan]] (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* [[:en:Wani clan|Wani clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] (Omi) – Descendants of [[:en:Takenouchi no Sukune|Takenouchi no Sukune]].

=== Nara Period ===
* [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]] (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}} through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', compiled by the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}} in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}} and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor {{ill|Saga|en|Saga|WD=Q180494|lt=Saga}} demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}, while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|en|Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|en|Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.
