'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}} through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', compiled by the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}} in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}} and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor {{ill|Saga|en|Saga|WD=Q180494|lt=Saga}} demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}, while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|en|Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.

* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}} through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', compiled by the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}} in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}} and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor {{ill|Saga|en|Saga|WD=Q180494|lt=Saga}} demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}, while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== See also ==
* {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}}
* {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}}
* {{ill|Imperial Descendant Sekke|ja|皇別摂家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Descendant Sekke|1=Kōbetsu sekke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581001}}
* {{ill|Ōke|en|Ōke|WD=Q864190|lt=Former Imperial Family}}
* {{ill|Branch family|ja|家制度#分家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Branch family|1=ie|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1149315}}
* {{ill|Cadet branch|ja|庶家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cadet branch|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}}
* {{ill|Royal descendants|ja|王孫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal descendants|1=Royal descendants|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18926038}}

* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime}}.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:皇別]][[ko:황별]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:皇別]][[ko:황별]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:皇別]][[ko:황별]]


'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', compiled by the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor {{ill|Saga|en|Saga|WD=Q180494|lt=Saga|1=Saga}} demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}}, while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}}. In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran|1=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike|1=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe|1=Konoe family}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō family}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa|1=Takatsukasa family}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime|1=Kamuyaimimi}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan|1=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan|1=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

== See also ==

* {{ill|Imperial Descendant Sekke|ja|皇別摂家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Descendant Sekke|1=Kōbetsu sekke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581001}}
* {{ill|Ōke|en|Ōke|WD=Q864190|lt=Former Imperial Family|1=Ōke}}
* {{ill|Branch family|ja|家制度#分家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Branch family|1=Ie (Japanese family system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1149315}}
* {{ill|Cadet branch|ja|庶家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cadet branch|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}}
* {{ill|Royal descendants|ja|王孫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal descendants|1=Q18926038|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18926038}}

Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe|1=Konoe family}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō family}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa|1=Takatsukasa family}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime|1=Kamuyaimimi}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan|1=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan|1=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.

'''Kōbetsu''' (皇別) refers to the classification of Japanese clans that descended from the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} through the {{ill|Demotion to subject status|ja|臣籍降下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demotion to subject status|qq=}} of collateral or cadet branches of the lineage after the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.{{sfn|Sato Yoshio|1994|p=36}}<ref>{{kotobank|Kōbetsu|Saeki Arikiyo, Encyclopedia of Japan}}</ref> This term was used in the genealogical register ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', compiled by the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]] during the [[Kōnin era]]. It categorized clans into three groups: {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}} (descendants of the [[Amatsukami]] and [[Kunitsukami]] deities), {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}} (imperial descendants), and {{ill|Shoban|ja|諸蕃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoban|qq=}} (descendants of immigrants from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}). The Kōbetsu classification was also applied during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} under the [[kazoku]] system.{{sfn|Ueno Hideharu|2001|pp=37-38}}

== Overview ==
Kōbetsu clans emerged in ancient Japan, with powerful noble families like the [[Omi clan|Omi]], [[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]], and [[Soga clan|Soga]] tracing their lineage back to the imperial family. Scholar [[Saeki Arikiyo]] suggested that the classification of clans by origin began around the [[Taika Reform]] and that the system of three categories—"Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban"—may have been formalized by the {{ill|Eight-Grade Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight-Grade Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} decree of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=214}} Kōbetsu clans often used the kabane title [[Omi (kabane)|Omi]], although there were many exceptions, such as Kōbetsu clans with the kabane title [[Muraji]], and vice versa.{{sfn|Shinokawa Ken|2007|pp=50-51}}

These aristocratic clans saw their influence decline with the rise of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and the consolidation of imperial power. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, most Kōbetsu clans had diminished to lower-Ranking [[kuge|nobility]] or local officials.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}} Only a few clans, such as the [[Abe clan|Abe]] and [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]], maintained prominence. The ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'', compiled in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], originally listed clans in the order "Shinbetsu, Kōbetsu, and Shoban." However, in [[:en:815 AD|815 AD]], this order was revised to "Kōbetsu, Shinbetsu, and Shoban," reflecting changing perceptions of their significance.{{sfn|Sugazawa Yoko|2001|p=211}}

In the Heian period, financial constraints and succession disputes led to the practice of granting non-heir children of emperors and their concubines surnames, thus lowering their imperial Rank. For example, in [[:en:814 AD|814 AD]], Emperor {{ill|Saga|en|Saga|WD=Q180494|lt=Saga|1=Saga}} demoted eight of his children to subject status under the surname [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]].{{sfn|Nakamura Midori|2015|pp=61}} These newly created [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] lines often struggled to maintain high status beyond the third generation. Most descendants either became low-Ranking nobles, provincial officials, or {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}}, while others disappeared from historical records.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|p=15}}

=== Medieval to Early Modern Period ===
Kōbetsu clans that moved to the provinces often became influential samurai families. Notable examples include the [[Taira clan|Kanmu Taira]] and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}}. In the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}}, the Kanmu Taira's {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} led the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran|1=Tengyō no Ran}}, and the {{ill|Heike|en|Heike|WD=Q5699231|lt=Heike|1=Heike}} (Ise Taira) temporarily dominated the imperial court during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The Seiwa Genji produced notable figures such as [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], who established the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, and {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. The [[Tokugawa clan]] also claimed descent from the Genji, although such claims were sometimes fabricated in later periods.{{sfn|Kuramoto Kazuhiro|2019|pp=124-132}}

=== Integration with Fujiwara Lineages ===
Some Fujiwara clans, originally classified as Shinbetsu, integrated imperial bloodlines through adoption or marriage. For example, the {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|WD=Q1781840|lt=Konoe|1=Konoe family}}, {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|WD=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō family}}, and {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa|1=Takatsukasa family}} branches were recognized as imperial descendants due to their close ties to the imperial family, occasionally earning the term "Kōbetsu Sesshō-ke" (imperial regent houses).{{sfn|Iwanami Yuko|2019|p=162}}

== Prominent Kōbetsu Clans ==
Below is a list of prominent Kōbetsu clans, categorized by period and kabane.

=== Asuka Period ===
* {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿蘇氏}} (Kimi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu through {{ill|Kamuyaimimi|en|Kamuyaimimi|WD=Q11588756|lt=Kamuyatatehime|1=Kamuyaimimi}}.
* {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|WD=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Jimmu.
* {{ill|Tenji clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenji clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan|1=Nitta clan}} (Sukune) – Descendants of Emperor Annei.
* {{ill|Wani clan|en|Wani clan|WD=Q11417873|lt=Wani clan|1=Wani clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōshō.
* {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kōgen.
* {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} (Omi) – Descendants of {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}.

=== Nara Period ===
* {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|WD=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}} (Sukune, later Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.
* {{ill|Fumi clan|ja|文室氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumi clan|1=Fun'ya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11499946}} (Manato) – Descendants of Emperor Tenmu.

=== Heian Period ===
* {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of Emperor Kanmu.
* {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} (Asomi) – Descendants of multiple emperors.
